content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 7, 2019

Amanda Guy
Deputy City Attorney
City of Carlsbad
1200 Carlsbad Village Dr.
Carlsbad, CA 92008

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 19-150

Dear Ms. Guy:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090, and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or the Public Contract Code.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Carlsbad (City) from contracting with a consultant engineering firm to provide 100% design plans for a project that the firm had initially provided 20% design plans for as part of an earlier contract?

CONCLUSION

No, because the provided facts do not indicate that the consultant engineering firm exerted influence over the City's contracting decisions.

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-150
Page No. 3

project to various other contractors. The current 20% design documents must be developed further
to provide 100% design construction documents for the project. The 20% design documents show
all floor plans and many architectural details (such as cabinet layout, equipment layout, furniture
layout, paint colors, and floor materials), but the addition of more detail, such as structural
connections, fire assembly details and engineering calculations, must be added so that a contractor
may build the project under a design-bid-build process. PAE has valuable project history and
knowledge that makes it desirable to continue to work with them. Additionally, a new consultant
would have to assume professional liability for PAE's 20% design work, which would create
additional cost.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 provides, "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district,
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Section 1090 is
concerned with financial interest, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public
officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of
their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.)

Interpreting the term "employees," as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court
has affirmed the long-standing rule (developed in case law) that independent contractors are not
categorically excluded from Section 1090, stating, "[l]iability under the statute can extend to
independent contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v.
Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233; see also, id. at p. 240, quoting Stigall, supra,
58 Cal.2d at p. 570 ("[S]ection 1090 liability extends only to independent contractors who can be
said to have been trusteed with 'transact[ing] on behalf of the Government.'").)

However, not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at
p. 240.) "An individual's status as an official under [Section 1090] turns on the extent to which the
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the
public trust." (Ibid., quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that exerts
"considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to Section
1090. (Hub City, supra, at pp. 1124-1125.)

Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are
removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency are not subject to Section
1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.)

The facts in this case demonstrate that PAE performed work of a limited, technical nature as
a consultant to the City, providing as-needed architecture and bridging tasks. The City would now
like to establish a new contract with PAE, essentially to complete the "design" process that PAE did
the initial work on pursuant to the parties' Master Agreement. Although PAE provided technical input in
providing the initial 20% design work for Task Nos. 2 and 5, you have indicated that PAE was not
consulted in any respect when the City decided to change the project delivery method and pursue a
100% design contract. There are also no facts indicating that PAE, in its role as a consultant to the
pursuant to the parties' Master Agreement, influenced the City's contracting decisions, or had the

File No. A-19-150
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

As part of the City's Capital Improvement Program (CIP), the City Council approved
Project No. 4715, titled Police and Fire Facilities—Carlsbad Safety Center Improvements (Project).
The Project will improve the City's Safety Center Facility by adding space under the existing salty
port for prisoner intake, and accommodating departmental growth by renovating the Police
Department's current dispatch area, sergeant's area and watch commander areas, and by renovating
open space on the second floor for new offices for the Fire Department. The Project also includes
structural updates to conform to code requirements, and the refurbishment or replacement of certain
mechanical units (e.g., HVAC, boiler, water heater).

On November 17, 2015, the City entered into Master Agreement No. CA1324 (Master
Agreement) with Pacific Architecture Engineering, Inc. (PAE) for a term of three years from
December 1, 2015 to November 30, 2018. The Master Agreement's total compensation was
established at a not-to-exceed amount of $300,000 to perform as-needed architecture and bridging
tasks related to space programming, conceptual design, bridging document development and design
administration services. The Master Agreement was not specific to the Project, but provided for the
issuance of Task Orders for various projects that required the contracted services.

On August 5, 2016, the City released Task No. 2 for the following services related to the
Carlsbad Safety Center Renovation: information gathering, stakeholder workshops and plan
development, programming services, and preparation of 20% design bridging documents, for a total
not-to-exceed amount of $100,000. Overall, Task No. 2 was intended to provide 20% design
bridging documents for the Safety Center's office space reconfigurations and building finishes
upgrades, in a manner largely designated by the City.

On June 27, 2017, the City issued Task No. 5, which addressed building infrastructure
replacement needs that became apparent to the City toward the end of the Task No. 2 programming
and space reconfiguration design work. Task No. 5 included the following services related to the
Carlsbad Safety Center Renovation: provide 20% design bridging documents to include certain
building shell modifications, mechanical, electrical, plumbing infrastructure, and site work,
reconfigure the HVAC and fire suppression design, and provide a cost estimate for the 20% design
work, for a total not-to-exceed amount of $89,830.

The City advertised the design-build request for proposals (RFP) on March 14, 2019, which
incorporated PAE's 20% design plans. However, the bid pricing in the proposals that the City
received far exceeded the City's construction budget for the project. Moreover, the highly
prescriptive nature of the work scope no longer matched the intent of design-build, and was better
suited to a design-bid-build delivery method. Accordingly, City staff would like to change the
project delivery method to design-bid-build, complete the Safety Center Renovation design work,
and rebid the project with 100% design plans. PAE did not participate in the City's decision to
change the project delivery method.

Assuming that all contracting requirements are satisfied, including potential City council
approval of the contract, the City would like to establish a new contract with PAE to provide the
100% design plans. The plans would not be used to develop a request for proposals on which PAE
could compete. Rather, the 100% design plans would be used to bid out the construction of the

File No. A-19-150
Page No. 4

potential to exert considerable influence on the City's contracting decisions. Accordingly, the City's
contracting with PAE to provide 100% design plans to be used in a new design-bid-build
procurement process for the Carlsbad Safety Center Building Renovation would not violate Section
1090.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in 
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Section 1090 is
